title: Grassmann tensor networks
authors: Jian-Gang Kong, Jia-Ji Zhu, Z. Y. Xie
abstract: Developing non-perturbative methods to reveal exotic properties of strongly correlated fermionic systems remains one of the most essential tasks of theoretical physics. Tensor network methods with Grassmann algebra offer powerful numerical tools for fermionic many-body systems in the coherent-state path-integral representation. Despite their vast potential for both condensed-matter and particle-physics communities, Grassmann tensor network methods are somewhat underexploited in practical simulations. In this work, we provide a detailed, self-contained introduction to Grassmann tensor network methods, from the basics of the Grassmann tensor operations to the Grassmannization of typical tensor network algorithms. Furthermore, the resulting Grassmann tensor network methods are validated in several interesting models in both particle physics and condensed matter physics.
summary: This paper provides a comprehensive introduction and a new unified framework for Grassmann tensor networks. It demonstrates how to adapt standard tensor network algorithms to handle fermionic statistics locally, offering a promising route for simulating complex 2D fermionic systems and lattice gauge theories.
main_problem: The paper addresses the need for efficient, non-perturbative numerical methods to study strongly correlated fermionic systems without the sign problem inherent in Monte Carlo simulations.
main_result: The authors establish a unified, self-contained framework for Grassmann tensor networks (GTNs) and demonstrate their validity through the 'Grassmannization' of standard algorithms like DMRG, TEBD, and CTMRG.
method: The method utilizes Grassmann algebra within a coherent-state path-integral representation, allowing for local handling of fermion exchange statistics and sign factors.
model_or_system: The framework is applied to various models including the Hubbard and t-J models, the Anderson impurity model, the honeycomb lattice t-V1-V2 model, and (1+1)D Gross-Neveu-Wilson models.
key_observables: Physical observables, partition functions, wave functions, particle density, and ground-state energy.
important_parameters: Bond dimension (D), environment dimension (chi), chemical potential (mu), and interaction strengths (t, J, V1, V2).
main_assumptions: The validity of the contraction depends on matching parity of shared indices; the ordering of Grassmann variables is assumed to match the index ordering of the tensors.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the SVD decomposition of a Grassmann tensor; Figure 9 and 10 show convergence of energy and particle density in the t-V1-V2 model compared to DMRG data.
paper_structure: The paper follows a pedagogical progression: starting with the basics of Grassmann algebra and operations, moving to tensor network representations and 'Grassmannization' of algorithms, and concluding with numerical validation on various physical models.
why_interesting: It provides a powerful new toolkit for simulating fermionic many-body dynamics and 2D systems by bypassing the non-local string operators of Jordan-Wigner transformations and the sign problem of Monte Carlo.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Decompose a rank-4 Grassmann tensor T to two rank-3 Grassmann tensors A and B by singular value decomposition, as expressed in Eqs. (43-44).
Figure 2: An effective (1+1)-dimensional lattice with coordinates (i, n), the Trotter direction and lattice direction are specified.

Figure 3: (a) The decomposition of the Grassmann exponents, as expressed in Eq. (124), at a given lattice site. (b) Definition of □i expressed in Eq. (126), integral of which leads to the (c) local Grassmann tensor T , as defined by Eq. (132).
Figure 4: The Grassmann generalization of the (a) left-canonical condition and (b) right-canonical condiction.

Figure 5: Ground-state properties of the half-filled Hubbard model given by Eq. (175) are computed via Grassmann DMRG, with Grassmann MPS bond dimension χ = 16. The Grassmann DMRG results are compared with the exact Bethe Ansatz solutions given by Eq. (177). (a) Finite-size convergence of the ground-state energy per site at on-site interaction strength U = 9. (b) Ground- state energy per site as a function of the on-site interaction strength U. (c, d) Site-resolved particle number distributions for spin-up (c) and spin-down (d) electrons on a chain of length m = 20.
